explicit_text: We moved to Fairview in May of 1973, coming from the Asheville area because we wanted to live in the country. Fairview felt safe and welcoming, especially with neighbors like Mrs. Peak who invited us for dinner the day we arrived. Over the years, I've seen Fairview change a lot, especially with more traffic and less of that close-knit community feeling. Despite the changes, I still appreciate living here and the sense of country living it offers, even if it's not quite the same as when we first moved.

explicit_text: Before moving to Fairview, we lived in Asheville, which was more of a town setting compared to the country life we wanted. Both my husband and I worked there; he at Ball Corporation and I at Sears. We decided to leave Asheville to find a quieter, more rural lifestyle, which is why we chose Fairview. Even though we moved away, being close to Asheville is still a positive because it keeps us near town amenities.

explicit_text: When we moved to Fairview in 1973, our home was on Old Fort Road, and we still live there today. Back then, it was a quiet two-lane road with very little traffic, and my daughter could safely walk to Sheet's Grocery nearby. Over time, Old Fort Road has expanded and become much busier and faster, making it unsafe for children to walk along it now. That road has really shown how much the area has changed since we first arrived.

explicit_text: Rupert Miller was my neighbor across the road when we moved to Fairview. I remember waking up many mornings to the sound of him calling, 'Soo cow, soo cow,' as he went out early to call his cows in for milking. It was a unique and charming part of country life that my daughter and I experienced, something that younger generations might not recognize today. His pasture was even affected during the flood of 1973 when Cane Creek overflowed.

explicit_text: Sheet's Grocery was a country store up the road on Old Fort Road where my daughter used to walk with her dog to buy candy and ice cream. It was a safe and friendly place, and the walk there was peaceful with hardly any traffic around. That store represented the close-knit, small-town feel of Fairview when we first moved here in 1973. Today, I doubt a child could safely make that walk because of the increased traffic.

explicit_text: When we first moved to Fairview, we attended Fairview Baptist Church on Sundays. The church community was very welcoming and embraced us with open arms, making us feel like we belonged right from the start. It was an important part of our integration into the community and contributed to the strong sense of belonging we experienced in those early days. That warmth and openness stood out to me as a highlight of living in Fairview.

explicit_text: My daughter started at Fairview Elementary School shortly after we moved here in 1973. She was new and didn't know anyone, but the teachers and students welcomed her warmly, which made the transition easier. The school was smaller than what she was used to in Asheville, and I was happy with the environment there. Overall, it was a positive experience for her and for us as parents.

explicit_text: My husband worked at Ball Corporation in Asheville, and it was the only job he ever had before retiring. He was employed there when we moved to Fairview in 1973, and that job helped support our family as we transitioned to country living. Ball Corporation was a jar factory, and his steady employment there was a big part of our life at that time.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Madison County, North Carolina, where my parents were farmers growing tobacco among other things. So, I really was a country girl from the start, which made moving to Fairview and living in the country a natural fit for me. My farming background gave me an appreciation for rural life that I carried with me when we relocated in 1973. It shaped much of my early life and values.

explicit_text: The flood of 1973 hit just one week after we moved to Fairview, on Memorial Day weekend, May 31st. Cane Creek overflowed its banks, flooding pastures and washing away bridges like Betty's Bridge. My neighbor Raymond Pressley waded water up to his neck trying to rescue his hunting dogs, and his big rock truck was swept away in the flood. It was a terrifying and unforgettable event that showed how quickly nature could change life here.

explicit_text: Memorial Day weekend in 1973 was when the devastating flood occurred just days after we moved to Fairview. It was a holiday weekend, and the flood came suddenly on Sunday night, causing severe damage to the area. That Memorial Day is etched in my memory because it marked a dramatic and frightening start to our life in Fairview. The community was deeply affected, and neighbors helped each other through the aftermath.

explicit_text: Raymond Pressley was my neighbor during the flood of 1973, and I remember him wading water up to his neck trying to save his hunting dogs. He was a rock mason and owned a big rock truck that was washed away in the floodwaters. His bravery and the challenges he faced during that disaster left a strong impression on me. Raymond's home became inaccessible because Betty's Bridge was destroyed, cutting off access across Cane Creek.

explicit_text: Betty's Bridge was a crossing over Cane Creek near my home in Fairview, and it was washed out during the flood of 1973. The destruction of the bridge cut off access to some homes, including my neighbor Raymond Pressley's house. Losing that bridge was a significant part of the flood's impact on our community, making travel and communication difficult. It was a vivid example of how the flood changed the landscape and daily life.

explicit_text: Cane Creek runs by my house in Fairview, and during the flood of 1973, it overflowed its banks dramatically. The floodwaters covered pastures, destroyed fences, and washed away Betty's Bridge, causing major disruption. It was the source of the flood's damage and the reason my neighbors had to deal with such difficult conditions. Cane Creek's flooding was a defining event in my early years living here.

explicit_text: Archie Peak was my neighbor across the road when we moved to Fairview, and during the 1973 flood, his fence was washed away by the rising waters. He was in his seventies and diabetic at the time, so he was worried about his cows escaping without the fence. My husband helped Archie’s son rebuild the fence since Archie wasn’t physically able to do it himself. The flood was a tough time for Archie and showed the strong bonds in our community as neighbors helped each other.
